title: Art Forum Speaker Series featuring Athena LaTocha
description:
The Department of Art & Design presents the Spring 2026 Art Forum series. Art Forum is a speaker series featuring artists, designers, art historians, curators, and art critics from around the world presenting their work and ideas in an open forum.  
   
 The next speaker in this engaging series is Athena LaTocha, an artist whose works on paper explore the relationship between human made and natural environments. She will discuss her practice and share insights into her latest work. 
   
 Athena LaTocha is an artist whose works on paper explore the relationship between human-made and natural worlds, in the wake of Earthworks artists from the 1960s and 1970s. The artist incorporates materials such as ink, lead, soils and wood, looking at mark-marking and displacement of materials made by industrial equipment and natural events. Her works are informed by her upbringing in the wilderness of Alaska. LaTocha’s process is about being immersed in these environments, while responding to the storied and, at times, traumatic histories that are rooted in place. 
   
 This speaker series is free and open to the public. 
   
 📅 Art Forum 
 Monday, 3/23/26  5:20pm-7pm 
 Calcia Hall, Room 140

title: Mushuk Nina - Fuego Nuevo -Expressing the Art and Language of the Andes
organization: Center for Latino Heritage & Spanish Language Excellence
description:
The name  Mushuk Nina  means New Fire. In Andean cosmology, fire is one of the four important elements that also include air, earth, and water.  The Mushuk Nina symbolizes the renewal of our energies,  is a Kichwa phrase meaning "New Year" representing the Andean New Year and a festival of renewal celebrated around the March equinox (typically March 21). It symbolizes the start of a new agricultural and spiritual cycle, focusing on purification, community gathering, and gratitude to Pachamama (Mother Earth).  
 In this event, Kichwa community leader, Atik Paguay, and acclaimed artist Raul Ayala, will lead an artistic and cultural event that will bring us from learning about this celebration and its meaning to participate to artistic creation led by Raul Ayala, to the construction of the "churu" (Snail) in the ground with music and stomping ceremony. 
 Join us!

title: GOVERNING BEYOND ELECTIONS: ACCOUNTABILITY, PROSPERITY AND THE AMERICAN ADMINISTRATIVE STATE
location: Presentation Hall (College of Communication and Media)
description: The emergence of a highly competent bureaucracy is typically under appreciated, if not ignored, as an ingredient in the emergence of the American experiment. While government administrators are often derided as inefficient or even obstructionist, technocrats are responsible for making government work and offer policy continuity and expertise across administrations. Still, the growth of a large permanent bureaucracy challenges the notion of accountability grounded in democratic elections. By tracing historical developments and contemporary debates, this lecture focuses on the centrality of government administrative capacity in the American experiment, focusing on the tensions and collaboration between elected officials and professional bureaucrats. We will consider the role of independent government agencies that were established with the idea of partitioning regulatory functions from partisan pressure.

title: Info Session for the new UG Certificate in Applied Spanish for the Professions - 3 tracks: Health professionals, Business, Justice Studies
organization: Center for Latino Heritage & Spanish Language Excellence
description:
Hi, 
 CLaSE - Center for Latino heritage and Spanish language Excellence - is launching a new program after listening to our students feedback and needs: 
 Undergraduate Certificate in Applied Spanish for the Professions 
 Launching in  Fall 2026,  this innovative program is designed for students who want to strengthen their Spanish language skills while preparing for careers that serve diverse communities. 
 Students can choose from three professional tracks tailored to their career interests: 
 
 
 Spanish for Health Professionals 
 
 
 Spanish for Justice Studies 
 
 
 Spanish for Business 
 
 
 Through practical coursework and real-world communication skills, students will learn how to use Spanish effectively in professional settings such as healthcare, legal and community services, and business environments. Use your elective courses, take 12 credits while completing the credits for your major and graduate with a title that certifies you to serve the Spanish speaking communities in your field. 
 In today’s multilingual society, the ability to communicate across languages and cultures is a powerful professional asset. This certificate helps students build career-ready language skills, cultural competence, and confidence while making a meaningful impact in Spanish-speaking communities. 
 During this info session, we will explain details and answer to all questions.

title: Weekly Food Recovery Initiative
description:
Join our weekly Food Recovery events, where dedicated volunteers and members gather to make a difference in our community. Each week, participants help collect perfectly good leftover food from Gourmet Dining and transport it to Blanton. There, the food is repackaged into individual microwavable containers and stocked in the food pantry, providing meals for those in need throughout the week.  
 To Participate : RSVP to attend the event each week. Meet in Blanton Hall Residence Hall Kitchenette in the Communal Area (Tell them you are working with Food Recovery), Sign in via Event Pass and your set! 
 Requirements: 
 
 Volunteers must be Food Safe Certified to participate. 
 Complete the certification by following the step-by-step instructions provided in this  link . 
 Once certified, email your certification to  foodrecoverynetwork@sgamsu.org  to confirm your eligibility to participate. 
 
 Your involvement helps reduce food waste and insecurity while supporting individuals in our community who are in need. We look forward to your participation! *Please Note: This event end time is dependent on the amount of food given. You can estimate for max of hour and a half for recoveries.

title: 2026 Class Gift Tabling - Information and Philanthropy Cord Pick-Up
description:
Each year, our graduating class participates in a proud Class Gift tradition where members of the class invest in the University by supporting areas of Montclair State University that are most important to them. We invite the Class of 2026 to come together to show your support! 
 All gifts to Montclair count! Make your gift to any area of campus you feel most passionate about – such as the Emergency Book Fund, The Red Hawk Pantry, your school or college, The Fund for Montclair State or athletic program. 
 Your gift will pay it forward for future Red Hawks and kick-start your philanthropic connection to Montclair. 
 
 Commemorate your class year! Make a gift of at least  $20.26  and you’ll receive your customized philanthropy cord to wear over your commencement robe.  
 For a gift of  $40 , celebrate this milestone with your family, friends and colleagues by having your message displayed on the screen at the Commencement you will be participating in, and receive a customized philanthropy cord. 
 
 How do I get my customized philanthropy cord? 
 Customized philanthropy cords will be available for pick-up: 
 
 Tuesday, October 28 from 1:00 – 3:00 p.m. in the Student Center Lobby 
 Thursday, November 20  from 1:00 – 3:00 p.m. in the Student Center Lobby 
 Tuesday, December 2 from 12:00 – 3:00 p.m. in the Student Center Lobby 
 Bloomfield Campus Graduation Fair: Wednesday, December 10th from 12:00 – 6:00 p.m. in Deacon’s Den, Talbott Hall  (for Bloomfield graduates only) 
 Montclair Campus Graduation Fairs: Thursday, December 11th from 10:00 a.m. – 7:00 p.m. in the Student Center Ballrooms  (for Montclair or Bloomfield graduates) 
 
 Graduates can also arrange to pick up their philanthropy cords in the Overlook Corporate Center, 150 Clove Road, Little Falls, NJ during business hours, Monday through Friday, 8:30 a.m. – 4:30 p.m. Philanthropy cords can also be mailed to those who cannot pick it up. The last day we will mail the cords is Monday, December 22, 2025 at 2:30 p.m. Please contact James Stewart, Associate Director of Annual Giving at stewartj@montclair.edu or 973-655-5328 to finalize options to get your cords for the Winter Commencement. 
 Every student has benefitted from donor generosity to areas of academic support, emergency funding for students and overall priorities to boost students’ success every day. Thank you for paying it forward! For more information, please contact James Stewart, Associate Director of Annual Giving, at 973-655-5328 or stewartj@montclair.edu.

title: CSAM Science Teaching Program - Program Meeting
description:
Please join us for two exciting activities! First, Ms. Jenny Pollack, Education Director for the Yogi Berra Museum & Learning Center will speak about employment opportunities to be a STEM and Sports educator to K-12 students who visit the Museum & Learning Center on class trips throughout the academic year. This is a terrific opportunity for CSAM science students who want to get experience teaching science to children - and it is right here on campus! The second activity will be a social 'build your own button' activity -- leave with a custom button of your own design for your backpack! 
   
 The  CSAM Science Teaching Program  holds  biweekly meetings  that are open, welcoming, and inclusive! Any student curious about science teaching is invited to join us. Each meeting highlights a theme related to science education, from classroom strategies to career pathways, and offers students opportunities to connect with mentors, explore internships, and gain real-world experience working with teachers and children. Students are encouraged to  bring a guest  who may also be interested in exploring science teaching. 
 These gatherings help us build a strong  community of support , where students encourage one another, share experiences, and access valuable opportunities such as  scholarships, paid internships, and mentoring  from advanced peers and in-service science teachers. Whether you are just beginning to explore the idea of teaching or already on the path toward certification, these meetings are a place to grow, learn, and belong.

title: Understanding Rights and Responsibilities— Immigration Policy
description:
NEW DATE FOR SESSIONS!  
 As immigration policies develop, it is best to stay educated. Know your rights and responsibilities! 
 Join the New Jersey Consortium for Immigrant Children for a webinar on key legal and policy issues impacting the Montclair community. Information will be provided on immigration statuses, policy updates, legal protections, judicial vs. administrative orders, and understanding your rights if encountering law enforcement. Sessions are open for students, family, faculty, and staff in both English and Spanish.  
 The New Jersey Consortium for Immigrant Children (NJCIC) is a state-wide legal and policy advocacy and education organization specializing in youth immigration. They provide objective legal resources, aid, and advice on immigration rights. 
   
 Student & Family English Session: March 25, 2:00-3:15:  https://us06web.zoom.us/webinar/register/WN_FxYlh_kDRkCYCoOhfmEKyA#/registration 
 Student & Family Spanish Session: April 9, 5:30-6:45:  https://us06web.zoom.us/j/82724283081?pwd=KzAjQw24vxqmzbURBXGmbBLV3V60m3.1

title: The DJ Academy: Music Matters 101
description:
DJing is one of the original elements of Hip Hop and is often considered the first. Hip Hop began in the Bronx during the 1970s at community block parties, where DJs used turntables to bring people together through music. Early pioneers like DJ Kool Herc introduced breakbeats by looping the most rhythmic parts of records, creating the foundation for breaking and shaping how dancers and MCs interacted with the music. 
 As Hip Hop evolved, DJing became the musical backbone of the culture. DJs provide the beats for MCing, set the tempo for dancers, and control the energy of Hip Hop spaces by reading the crowd and responding in real time. Through techniques like scratching, cutting, and beat juggling, DJs helped define Hip Hop’s sound while preserving its history and passing down cultural knowledge. 
 This class introduces the basics of DJing and how music shapes the dance experience. DJ Bascis will explain core concepts such as  tempo, rhythm, and song structure , helping students understand how music guides movement and energy on the dance floor. 
 Participants will learn how DJs choose music, create smooth transitions, and adjust the vibe based on the room.  
 Through simple examples and live demonstrations, students will build confidence in listening to music with intention. This can bewhether they are dancing, freestyling, or interested in DJing themselves. 
 This class is designed for  beginners and college students , with no prior DJ or music experience required.

title: Before the Flame : Powerful Voices in Action
description: Before the Flame: Voices in Action  is an annual event organized by the Student Government Association in honor of Women’s History Month. In its first installment, this event will take place prior to the Center for Student Leadership & Engagement’s annual Igniting the Flame program. The event will feature faculty speakers from various disciplines who will share their experiences, passions, and perspectives on advocacy, communication, and community leadership. Through a panel discussion and interactive breakout workshops, attendees will explore strategies for navigating conflict, facilitating repair, and advocating for themselves and others both on and off campus. While the event centers the empowerment and experiences of women, who remain an oppressed class in society, it is  free and open to all staff, faculty, and students . Participants will have opportunities to ask questions, engage in guided discussions, and practice real-world communication strategies that support equity, accountability, and collective growth.

title: Weekly Food Recovery Initiative
description:
Join our weekly Food Recovery events, where dedicated volunteers and members gather to make a difference in our community. Each week, participants help collect perfectly good leftover food from Gourmet Dining and transport it to Blanton. There, the food is repackaged into individual microwavable containers and stocked in the food pantry, providing meals for those in need throughout the week.  
 To Participate : RSVP to attend the event each week. Meet in Blanton Hall Residence Hall Kitchenette in the Communal Area (Tell them you are working with Food Recovery), Sign in via Event Pass and your set! 
 Requirements: 
 
 Volunteers must be Food Safe Certified to participate. 
 Complete the certification by following the step-by-step instructions provided in this  link . 
 Once certified, email your certification to  foodrecoverynetwork@sgamsu.org  to confirm your eligibility to participate. 
 
 Your involvement helps reduce food waste and insecurity while supporting individuals in our community who are in need. We look forward to your participation! *Please Note: This event end time is dependent on the amount of food given. You can estimate for max of hour and a half for recoveries.

The Department of Art & Design invites you to  From the Basement , the BFA Visual Communications Design 2026 Graduate Exhibition. Join us at the Finley Gallery from March 25 to April 7 to experience a dynamic showcase of work by graduating VCDS students, featuring posters, motion graphics, packaging, editorial, and more. With the opening reception on Friday, March 27, 4 to 6pm. 
   
 Venture into the world of graphic design at the VCDS 2026 graduate exhibition,  From the Basement.  This exhibition highlights the work of graduating Visual Communication Design students and the projects they developed throughout their time in the VCDS BFA program. A range of mediums will be featured, including posters, motion graphics, packaging, editorial, and more! Together, these works reflect the unique styles and creative voices of the class.

 Jalen Aguirre, Ysabella De Leon, Brett Diese, Domenic Falivene, Anna Fronczkowski, Tori Gallagher, Camila Garcia, Robert Gaskill, Britany Giraldo, David Guerra, Audrey Hitchens, Natalia Kurpiewski, Paolo La Rosa, Izzy Latella, Katie Mascuch, Caitlin Mendoza, Evan Okun, Erika Oliva, Julia Oliveri, Christian Pajaro, Nicole Ricci, Christopher Rizzi, Casey Ryu, Tom Sheridan, Michael Shimabukuro, Gabby Trezza, Maral Tutunjian, and Dogan Uludogan

title: Database Research Strategies and Advanced Search Tips
description:
In this workshop, learn some practical ways to increase the effectiveness of your database searches. As databases have become larger, searching has actually become more difficult to do well without knowing more about how these databases are being developed and managed today. We’ll discuss using Boolean searching tools to manipulate search results, how to engage with database thesauri and a controlled vocabulary, linked subject headings, setting up search alerts, and more. 
 Register Here:  https://montclair.libcal.com/event/16500101

title: Faith and Fear: Religion and Anti-Immigrant Discourse in the U.S.
organization: College of Humanities and Social Sciences (CHSS)
description:
Immigrant policy in the United States is increasingly shaped not only by the law and enforcement, but by religious rhetoric which frames exclusion as moral necessity. The Department of Religion hosts Dr. Thomas Lecaque for a lecture examining how faith-based symbolism and Christian imagery are mobilized to shield aggressive immigration enforcement, including the expanding justification and visibility of ICE. By situating these developments within broader U.S. religious and political histories, the lecture critically considers how religion can sacralize state power, normalize fear, and reshape public understandings of belonging, citizenship, race, and human dignity. 
   
 This event will take place on Zoom.  Please register here

title: BFA Visual Communications Design Graduate Exhibition
description:
The Department of Art & Design invites you to  From the Basement , the BFA Visual Communications Design 2026 Graduate Exhibition. Join us at the Finley Gallery from March 25 to April 7 to experience a dynamic showcase of work by graduating VCDS students, featuring posters, motion graphics, packaging, editorial, and more. With the opening reception on Friday, March 27, 4 to 6pm. 
   
 Venture into the world of graphic design at the VCDS 2026 graduate exhibition,  From the Basement.  This exhibition highlights the work of graduating Visual Communication Design students and the projects they developed throughout their time in the VCDS BFA program. A range of mediums will be featured, including posters, motion graphics, packaging, editorial, and more! Together, these works reflect the unique styles and creative voices of the class. 
   
 Featured Designers: 
 Jalen Aguirre, Ysabella De Leon, Brett Diese, Domenic Falivene, Anna Fronczkowski, Tori Gallagher, Camila Garcia, Robert Gaskill, Britany Giraldo, David Guerra, Audrey Hitchens, Natalia Kurpiewski, Paolo La Rosa, Izzy Latella, Katie Mascuch, Caitlin Mendoza, Evan Okun, Erika Oliva, Julia Oliveri, Christian Pajaro, Nicole Ricci, Christopher Rizzi, Casey Ryu, Tom Sheridan, Michael Shimabukuro, Gabby Trezza, Maral Tutunjian, and Dogan Uludogan 
   
 BFA Visual Communications Design Graduate Exhibition 
 Exhibition dates: March 25–April 7, 2026 
 Opening Reception: Friday, March 27, 4–6pm 
 Finley Gallery, Finley Hall 
   
 Come meet the designers and celebrate their work! Support the next generation of designers as they transition from academia into the professional world.

title: Champions for the Arts with Donna Walker-Kuhne
location: Presentation Hall, College of Media and Communication Building
description:
With the loss of "third spaces," an increase in online socialization, and the dwindling of social clubs and groups, we are increasingly seeking a way to combat loneliness and isolation. Through their ability to bring people together in person and to connect our hearts to beat as one, the arts remain a powerful way to bring us closer to those around us. Donna Walker-Kuhne, the nation's foremost expert in audience development, leads an inspiring discussion on how artistic communities can meaningfully connect, grow inclusive audiences, and create a more vibrant artistic world. In this open and inviting conversation, she shares concrete examples of successful audience outreach from her extensive field experience, as well as spotlighting young Champions for the Arts in the Montclair State University community. They're ready to pledge their commitment to forging dynamic strategies to strengthen the arts. Are you ready to join them? 
   
 Donna Walker-Kuhne is an award-winning thought leader, lawyer, scholar, educator, writer, and strategist for community engagement, audience development, and social justice. Acknowledged as the nation‘s foremost expert on audience development by the Arts & Business Council, she has worked with NJPAC, Dance Theatre of Harlem, Alvin Ailey American Dance Theater, The Public Theater, WNYC Radio, The Apollo Theater, and more, and consulted on over 22 Broadway productions. As founder and president of Walker International Communications Group, Inc., she has raised over $23 million in earned income by promoting the arts to diverse communities. Walker is the author of Invitation to the Party, Building Bridges to Arts, Culture, and Community, and Champions for the Arts, Successful Strategies for Engaging Dynamic Communities. 
   
 Tickets to this event are free for all, not just students! To join us,  reserve a seat online here  or at the Alexander Kasser Theatre box office, Monday through Friday between 12 and 6 PM.

description:
Cru is a caring community passionate about connecting people to Jesus Christ!  Join us in learning together on Who Christ is, building a personal relationship with Him, and joining a community filled with love and open arms!  
 This semester, we will be focusing on the theme  "Identity in Christ."  We pray that our students will learn more about who they are in Christ, and look at some examples of people in the Bible where God revealed their true identity in Christ! 
 " And they devoted themselves to the apostles' teaching and the fellowship, to the breaking of bread and the prayers.   And awe came upon every soul, and many wonders and signs were being done through the apostles.   And all who believed were together and had all things in common.   And they were selling their possessions and belongings and distributing the proceeds to all, as any had need.   And day by day, attending the temple together and breaking bread in their homes, they received their food with glad and generous hearts,  praising God and having favor with all the people. And the Lord added to their number day by day those who were being saved." - Acts 2:42-47

title: CALI SCHOOL OF MUSIC | Colin Carr Cello Masterclass
description:
Colin Carr Cello Masterclass 
 Internationally acclaimed cellist  Colin Carr,  renowned as a soloist, chamber musician, recording artist, and dedicated teacher, will visit the Cali School for a masterclass and share his insights into cello performance and musical artistry. 
 Bio 
 Colin Carr  appears throughout the world as a soloist, chamber musician, recording artist, and teacher. He has played with major orchestras worldwide, including the Royal Concertgebouw Orchestra, The Philharmonia, Royal Philharmonic, BBC Symphony, the orchestras of Chicago, Los Angeles, Washington, Philadelphia, Montréal and all the major orchestras of Australia and New Zealand. Conductors with whom he has worked include Rattle, Gergiev, Dutoit, Elder, Skrowasczewski and Marriner. Chamber music plays an important role in his musical life. He is a frequent visitor to international festivals and has appeared often as a guest with the Guarneri and Emerson string quartets and with New York's Chamber Music Society of Lincoln Center. He has held teaching positions at the New England Conservatory and the Royal Academy of Music. St John’s College, Oxford created the post of “Musician in Residence” for him. Since 2002 he been a professor at Stony Brook University in New York.

description:
The Department of Art & Design invites you to  From the Basement , the BFA Visual Communications Design 2026 Graduate Exhibition. Join us at the Finley Gallery from March 25 to April 7 to experience a dynamic showcase of work by graduating VCDS students, featuring posters, motion graphics, packaging, editorial, and more. title: March Coffee House Night RA+P
description:
Coffeehouse is our live music event where students can sign up to perform in Russ Lounge. Whether you’re playing, singing, or just coming to hang out, it’s always a great night of creativity and community. 
 The goal of the event is to turn Russ Lounge into a performance space that can accommodate an audience, but it’s also a hands-on opportunity to learn how to set up a performance from start to finish. We will be mixing on a Midas M32R, so it's also an opportunity to learn how digital mixers for live audio work. All that prep work culminates in a show featuring Montclair State students from 7 PM to 9:15 PM. We go through the process of setup, performance, and breakdown. IF this interests you, please fill out this form for more information on how to join the crew. 
 See you soon, stay groovy!! 
 All students are welcome, commuters need to be signed in a with a resident following Residence Life guest registration procedures.

title: CALI SCHOOL OF MUSIC | The Reawakening of the Gods - Musical Reading
description:
The Reawakening of the Gods 
 A Public Reading of a New Musical 
 Friday, March 27, 2026 at 8:00 PM Leshowitz Recital Hall John J. Cali School of Music Montclair State University 
 The John J. Cali School of Music, in collaboration with the Inserra Chair in Italian and Italian American Studies and the Musical Theatre Program, presents a public reading of  The Reawakening of the Gods , a new musical written and directed by Marco Savatteri and produced in partnership with Savatteri Produzioni. 
 This staged reading will be interpreted by students from the Cali School’s Musical Theatre Program (CART), with assistance from Toti Maria Geraci and accompaniment by David Fraley. The event also features brief talks by Teresa Fiore, an English translation by Brianna Coppolino, and reflections on the Savatteri internship experience by Mia Grizzuti. 
 Blending classical themes with contemporary musical storytelling,  The Reawakening of the Gods  offers audiences an opportunity to experience the development of new work while engaging with an international creative collaboration. This special event highlights the Cali School’s commitment to interdisciplinary performance, global artistic exchange, and student-centered creative practice. 
 Admission:  Free and open to the public More information:  tinyurl.com/ReadingReawakening

title: Turkey Mountain Hike (Easy)
description:
Looking  for  a  nice  day  out  with  your  friends?  Join  us  on  an easy  hike  at Turkey Mountain in   Montville Township . This  5.4  mile  hike  offers multiple viewpoints, a waterfall, and an area to explore ruins of an old stone home! 
 
 How do I sign-up? 
 
 Register in the Student Recreation Center main office or over the phone (973-655-3340). 
 Cost for Students  $10 ; Faculty/Staffa $20 
 
   
 Where and when? 
 
 Sunday, March 29 2026 
 10 a.m. - 5 p.m. 
 Begins at Student Recreation Center 
 
   
 What to bring:  Water, Lunch, and Boots or Sneakers 
   
 Potential Hazards:  Unpredictable weather changes, sunburn/heat exhaustion, limited vision, dehydration, allergic reactions/poisonous plants, insect bites, and injury by running, including sprains, lacerations, abrasions and fractures.

title: CALI SCHOOL OF MUSIC: Piano Masterclass with Carolyn Enger
description:
Piano Masterclass with Carolyn Enger 
 Internationally celebrated American pianist  Carolyn Enger  has gained critical acclaim for her exquisite lyrical playing, as well as her deeply felt interpretations.  The  New York Times   selected her Naxos recording of intimate Ned Rorem miniatures,  Piano Album I & Six Friends , as one of the newspaper’s “Best In Classical Recordings.” 
 Her latest recording,  Resonating Earth  was released in September 2024 to glowing praise: “a thoughtful programme…enthralling” (Nigel Simone,  International Piano ), “lovingly performed…a beautiful, meaningful recital” (Steve Smith,  Night After Night , “sensitively performed and intelligently curated” (Frances Wilson,  Interlude ), and has been streamed over 500,000 times on Apple Music.

title: Free Swim Lessons *Intermediate*
description:
Take your swimming to the next level! Get introduced to new strokes or work on the ones you already know. These lessons are for swimmers who are a little more comfortable in the water but still want to improve their technique and expand what skills they know.  
   
 Skills Focus: 
 
 Swimming unassisted on your front and back  
 Improve treading (in the deep end) 
 Improving breath control in the water 
 Learn alternate kicks (Dolphin Kick) 
 Introduction to and/or improving your breaststroke 
 Swimming laps 
 Introduction to open and flip turns 
 Introduction to Butterfly 
 
 How do I Sign-up? 
 
 Registration is not required 
 FREE with a valid Montclair State ID 
 
 When and Where? 
 
 Monday: 1-2PM & 5-6PM 
 Student Recreation Center - Pool

title: Free Swim Lessons *Beginner*
description:
Brief Description:  
 Never swam before? No problem! These lessons will give you the important foundational skills in swimming and help you be more comfortable in the pool. Learn how to use proper breath control, float, and swim with a kickboard.  
 Skills Focus: 
 
 Being comfortable in the water 
 Learning how to float on your back and front 
 Learning how to kick off the wall with a kickboard 
 Introducing proper breath control in the water 
 Learning how to tread water 
 Learn alternate kicks (Froggy Kick) 
 Being comfortable in the deep end  
 Introducing arms to your strokes

title: Patriotic Trivia (Bloomfield Campus)
description:
Stars, stripes, and some serious smarts! ????? Join us for  Patriotic Trivia , a fast-paced, fun-filled event where you can test your knowledge of U.S. history, government, iconic moments, and all things red, white, and blue! 
 Whether you’re a history buff or just in it for the fun, this is your chance to show off what you know, learn something new, and enjoy some friendly competition with fellow students, faculty, and staff. 
 From the Constitution to pop culture, the questions will keep you guessing—and prizes are up for grabs! Come solo or bring a team, and get ready for an exciting celebration of civic knowledge and national pride. 
 Think you’ve got what it takes? Let’s find out—one trivia question at a time! Stop by, enjoy some pizza and you might even win some really cool prizes

 Whether you’re new to DECA or preparing to compete, our GBMs are designed to help you build confidence, think critically, and develop leadership skills in a collaborative environment. 
 All majors and experience levels are welcome.

title: Bench Press and Squat Competition
description:
Spring Strength Shadow! Welcome back to our annual Bench Press & Squat Competition. Come and test power and show your gains. Each event will feature 5 reps max. Whether you're chasing PRs or just want to flex for fun, this event is for you! Bring your friends and your lifting straps, and see who can lift their way to the top prize! 
 How do I Sign-up? 
 
 Registration is not required 
 FREE with a valid Montclair State ID 
 
 When and Where? 
 
 Data: March 30th 
 Time: 3pm - 6pm 
 Student Recreation Center, Plae Room 
 
 How are results scored 
 
 Maximum 5 repetitions  
 1 attempt per lift after a warm up set 
 Scoring calculation: Weight lifted x repetitions divided by body weight  
 Final score will be a total of both lifts combined  
 
 Prizes :  
 
 Will be given to the top 3 winners  
 Tier 1: Beat Headphone ,  Fitness Watch ,  Air Fryer  $100-$200 
 Tier 2:  Massage Gun ,  Meal Prep Kit ,  Pilates Kit  $50-$100 
 Tier 3:  Foam Roller Set ,  Liquid IV ,  Owala Water Bottle  $20-$50 
 
 Free T-shirts - limited supply so show up early to claim one!

title: BFA Visual Communications Design Graduate Exhibition
description:
The Department of Art & Design invites you to  From the Basement , the BFA Visual Communications Design 2026 Graduate Exhibition. Join us at the Finley Gallery from March 25 to April 7 to experience a dynamic showcase of work by graduating VCDS students, featuring posters, motion graphics, packaging, editorial, and more. With the opening reception on Friday, March 27, 4 to 6pm. 
   
 Venture into the world of graphic design at the VCDS 2026 graduate exhibition,  From the Basement.  This exhibition highlights the work of graduating Visual Communication Design students and the projects they developed throughout their time in the VCDS BFA program. A range of mediums will be featured, including posters, motion graphics, packaging, editorial, and more! Together, these works reflect the unique styles and creative voices of the class. 
   
 Featured Designers: 
 Jalen Aguirre, Ysabella De Leon, Brett Diese, Domenic Falivene, Anna Fronczkowski, Tori Gallagher, Camila Garcia, Robert Gaskill, Britany Giraldo, David Guerra, Audrey Hitchens, Natalia Kurpiewski, Paolo La Rosa, Izzy Latella, Katie Mascuch, Caitlin Mendoza, Evan Okun, Erika Oliva, Julia Oliveri, Christian Pajaro, Nicole Ricci, Christopher Rizzi, Casey Ryu, Tom Sheridan, Michael Shimabukuro, Gabby Trezza, Maral Tutunjian, and Dogan Uludogan 
   
 BFA Visual Communications Design Graduate Exhibition 
 Exhibition dates: March 25–April 7, 2026 
 Opening Reception: Friday, March 27, 4–6pm 
 Finley Gallery, Finley Hall 
   
 Come meet the designers and celebrate their work! Support the next generation of designers as they transition from academia into the professional world.

description: A New Member Education (NME) meeting is a structured gathering designed to help new members learn about Tri Sigma’s history, values, traditions, and expectations. During these meetings, new members build connections with one another, develop leadership and personal growth skills, and gain a deeper understanding of what it means to be a lifelong member of the organization. NME meetings create a supportive environment where new members can ask questions, reflect on their experiences, and prepare for initiation while strengthening their sense of belonging within the chapter.

description:

Whether you’re looking to meet other students who love to lift, pursue new fitness goals, or simply feel more at home in the weight room, upLIFT offers a supportive community and a dedicated space to grow. location: Montclair State University, University Hall Conference Center, 7th Floor
description:

Panelists will include community responders, leaders in the CVI field, experts in mental health and crisis response, policy advocates, and research experts.

title: AI Distinguished Speaker Series: The AI-Ready Campus: From Pockets of Innovation to a Coherent Institutional Strategy
organization: College of Humanities and Social Sciences (CHSS)
location: Presentation Hall, College of Communication and Media
description:
The College of Humanities and Social Sciences is launching a new AI  Distinguished Speaker Series —a yearlong exploration of how artificial intelligence is reshaping human systems, knowledge, and society. Designed to be inclusive and engaging, the series will bring thought leaders to campus who not only explain emerging technologies but also invite students and the wider community into critical conversations about their impact. By blending expert insight with student-centered formats, the series aims to spark new ways of learning, questioning, and participating in AI’s future. 
 The AI-Ready Campus: From Pockets of Innovation to a Coherent Institutional Strategy 
 Across higher education, the adoption of generative AI has been rapid and revolutionary. While many faculty and staff are exploring these powerful new tools, this energy is often siloed, leading to a campus of “AI pockets” rather than a truly AI-ready institution. The next great challenge is moving from this phase of enthusiastic but fragmented experimentation to one of deliberate, strategic, and holistic integration. How do we build an institutional AI strategy that is more than just a collection of policies and tools? 
 Register to Attend

title: The DJ Academy: Music Matters 101
description:
DJing is one of the original elements of Hip Hop and is often considered the first. Hip Hop began in the Bronx during the 1970s at community block parties, where DJs used turntables to bring people together through music. Early pioneers like DJ Kool Herc introduced breakbeats by looping the most rhythmic parts of records, creating the foundation for breaking and shaping how dancers and MCs interacted with the music. 
 As Hip Hop evolved, DJing became the musical backbone of the culture. DJs provide the beats for MCing, set the tempo for dancers, and control the energy of Hip Hop spaces by reading the crowd and responding in real time. Through techniques like scratching, cutting, and beat juggling, DJs helped define Hip Hop’s sound while preserving its history and passing down cultural knowledge. 
 This class introduces the basics of DJing and how music shapes the dance experience. DJ Bascis will explain core concepts such as  tempo, rhythm, and song structure , helping students understand how music guides movement and energy on the dance floor. 
 Participants will learn how DJs choose music, create smooth transitions, and adjust the vibe based on the room.  
 Through simple examples and live demonstrations, students will build confidence in listening to music with intention. This can bewhether they are dancing, freestyling, or interested in DJing themselves. 
 This class is designed for  beginners and college students , with no prior DJ or music experience required.

At each meeting, we'll engage in lively discussions, learn about Africa’s diverse history, traditions, and current events, and empower each other to understand and embrace our heritage. Whether you're looking to deepen your cultural knowledge, make meaningful connections, or simply have fun with fellow students who share a passion for Africa and global cultures, this is the place to be! Let’s build a supportive community, amplify African voices on campus, and create lasting friendships. All are welcome, come, learn, share, and connect!

title: CALI SCHOOL OF MUSIC: Ashley Hall-Tighe Masterclass
description:
Ashley  Hall-Tighe Masterclass Beyond the Instrument:  Building Mental Resilience and Finding Joy in Your Creative Life 
 In this interactive workshop, life coach and professional musician  Ashley Hall-Tighe  guides participants through practical strategies for navigating performance anxiety, perfectionism, and the ever-present inner critic. Through reflection and discussion, musicians will learn tools to cultivate mental resilience, quiet negative self-talk, and reconnect with the values and moments of joy that first inspired their creative journey. 
 Ashley Hall-Tighe  is an internationally acclaimed trumpet soloist, chamber musician, and educator known for her warm tone, expressive artistry, and engaging performances. Principal trumpet of the Cincinnati Chamber Orchestra since 2008, she recently toured worldwide with Canadian Brass. A dedicated teacher and Yamaha performing artist, she serves on the faculty of the University of Cincinnati College-Conservatory of Music. 
 All are welcome.  Reservations are not necessary.

title: THE EPIC OF ANCIENT IRELAND
organization: College of Humanities and Social Sciences (CHSS)
description:
THE EPIC OF ANCIENT IRELAND 
 Dr. Matthieu Boyd Professor of Literature Fairleigh Dickinson University 
   
 Dr. Matthieu Boyd will be discussing his new translation of the Irish epic "Táin Bó Cúailnge" (Broadview Press, 2025), an important development in ancient and medieval Celtic Studies and the study of myth and epic. Once touted as "a window on the Iron Age," the Táin is considered an invaluable source of information on Pagan and early Christian Irish society and its literature. 
 WED., APRIL 1 6:30 – 8:30 PM DICKSON HALL, ROOM 178 
   
 Please contact Professor Glen Robert Gill at  gillg@montclair.edu for additional details. 
   
 MONTCLAIR STATE UNIVERSITY Classics and General Humanities College of Humanities and Social Sciences

location: University Hall 1030 - Charlotte Roome Shotmeyer Lecture Hall
description: A New Member Education (NME) meeting is a structured gathering designed to help new members learn about Tri Sigma’s history, values, traditions, and expectations. During these meetings, new members build connections with one another, develop leadership and personal growth skills, and gain a deeper understanding of what it means to be a lifelong member of the organization. NME meetings create a supportive environment where new members can ask questions, reflect on their experiences, and prepare for initiation while strengthening their sense of belonging within the chapter.

title: Sin Olvidar: Climbing Ladders, Carrying Culture
organization: Sigma Lambda Beta International Fraternity Inc.
location: University Hall 1020 - Bard-Blount Family Lecture Hall
description:
Many Latino professionals are navigating spaces our families were never given access to-learning workplace norms, financial literacy, and career strategies without the benefit of generational guidance. For those who come from low-income or historically underserved communities, success often means figuring out systems that were never designed with us in mind. 
 This conversation explores what it means to be first-generation professionals, to build skills and stability that we may not have inherited, and to confront the pressure to assimilate in order to succeed More importantly, it asks: how do we grow in corporate and professional spaces without losing our identity, our community, and the values that shaped us? 
 Join us for an honest discussion on navigating professional environments, closing generational knowledge gaps, building financial and career confidence, and staying rooted in culture while advancing in spaces where representation is still growing. This event is about redefining success-not as leaving where we come from behind, but as carrying it with us as we create new pathways for those who will follow.

title: Spring Interdisciplinary Showcase
organization: College of Humanities and Social Sciences (CHSS)
description:
Building on the success from the fall showcase, faculty and students from across the University will come together again in April for the Interdisciplinary Showcase, an event celebrating innovative scholarship, creative projects, and student collaborations that exemplify the theme of Interdisciplinary Work in Action. The showcase explores how interdisciplinarity can enact meaningful changes in our social landscapes through our work, ideas, and actions, from the individual and community level to the societal and global scale. 
 The showcase features 13 presentations spanning the humanities, social sciences, communication, education and business. Projects examine issues such as equity and inclusion, economic inequality, human trafficking, and community well-being, alongside interdisciplinary work in media, culture, and religion. Presentations also highlight collaborations that use data analysis, advocacy, and community engagement to better understand and respond to complex social challenges. 
 Each project highlights how interdisciplinary collaboration fosters new insights, community partnerships, and real-world impact. From community-engaged research to program design and creative pedagogy, the presentations demonstrate how scholars and students expand the boundaries of traditional disciplines to address complex challenges. 
 The Interdisciplinary Showcase invites colleagues from across the university to explore ideas, connect with peers, and spark new collaborations that advance Montclair’s mission of inclusive innovation. 
 Register to Attend

title: Design Your Day: Discover Your Direction
description:
Not sure what to major in or where you're headed after college? Let’s change that—starting with the kind of  day  you want to have. In this hands-on, creative workshop, you’ll: 
 1) Visualize your ideal workday—where you are, who you’re with, and what you’re doing. 
 2) Identify your key work-life preferences, like flexibility, collaboration, or creativity. 
 3) Explore majors and careers that align with your vision through UC’s AI-powered  Major Mentor , a tool that matches your preferences to Montclair majors and career paths. 
 This event is open to all University College (pre-major) students. RSVP is required.  There will be complimentary food.

title: Free Swim Lessons *Beginner*
description:
Never swam before? No problem! These lessons will give you the important foundational skills in swimming and help you be more comfortable in the pool. Learn how to use proper breath control, float, and swim with a kickboard.  
 Skills Focus: 
 
 Being comfortable in the water 
 Learning how to float on your back and front 
 Learning how to kick off the wall with a kickboard 
 Introducing proper breath control in the water 
 Learning how to tread water 
 Learn alternate kicks (Froggy Kick) 
 Being comfortable in the deep end  
 Introducing arms to your strokes 
 
 How do I Sign-up? 
 
 Registration is not required 
 FREE with a valid Montclair State ID 
 
 When and Where? 
 
 Thursday: 4-5PM 
 Student Recreation Center - Pool

title: Free Swim Lessons *Intermediate*
description:
Take your swimming to the next level! Get introduced to new strokes or work on the ones you already know. These lessons are for swimmers who are a little more comfortable in the water but still want to improve their technique and expand what skills they know.  
   
 Skills Focus: 
 
 Swimming unassisted on your front and back  
 Improve treading (in the deep end) 
 Improving breath control in the water 
 Learn alternate kicks (Dolphin Kick) 
 Introduction to and/or improving your breaststroke 
 Swimming laps 
 Introduction to open and flip turns 
 Introduction to Butterfly 
 
 How do I Sign-up? 
 
 Registration is not required 
 FREE with a valid Montclair State ID

title: BFA Visual Communications Design Graduate Exhibition
description:
The Department of Art & Design invites you to  From the Basement , the BFA Visual Communications Design 2026 Graduate Exhibition. Join us at the Finley Gallery from March 25 to April 7 to experience a dynamic showcase of work by graduating VCDS students, featuring posters, motion graphics, packaging, editorial, and more. With the opening reception on Friday, March 27, 4 to 6pm. 
   
 Venture into the world of graphic design at the VCDS 2026 graduate exhibition,  From the Basement.  This exhibition highlights the work of graduating Visual Communication Design students and the projects they developed throughout their time in the VCDS BFA program. A range of mediums will be featured, including posters, motion graphics, packaging, editorial, and more! Together, these works reflect the unique styles and creative voices of the class. 
   
 Featured Designers: 
 Jalen Aguirre, Ysabella De Leon, Brett Diese, Domenic Falivene, Anna Fronczkowski, Tori Gallagher, Camila Garcia, Robert Gaskill, Britany Giraldo, David Guerra, Audrey Hitchens, Natalia Kurpiewski, Paolo La Rosa, Izzy Latella, Katie Mascuch, Caitlin Mendoza, Evan Okun, Erika Oliva, Julia Oliveri, Christian Pajaro, Nicole Ricci, Christopher Rizzi, Casey Ryu, Tom Sheridan, Michael Shimabukuro, Gabby Trezza, Maral Tutunjian, and Dogan Uludogan 
   
 BFA Visual Communications Design Graduate Exhibition 
 Exhibition dates: March 25–April 7, 2026 
 Opening Reception: Friday, March 27, 4–6pm 
 Finley Gallery, Finley Hall 
   
 Come meet the designers and celebrate their work! Support the next generation of designers as they transition from academia into the professional world.

title: Why SLB?
organization: Sigma Lambda Beta International Fraternity Inc.
description:
Join Sigma Lambda Beta International Fraternity Inc. for an open panel discussion where five brothers share their personal journeys, backgrounds, and experiences within Greek life. This event is designed to give students an authentic look into what it means to be part of SLB — beyond stereotypes and surface-level perceptions. 
 Panelists will speak on their upbringing, what led them to explore Greek life, why they chose Sigma Lambda Beta, and how brotherhood has shaped their identity, leadership, and college experience. Attendees will also have the opportunity to ask questions and connect with brothers afterward in an informal setting. 
 Whether you’re curious about culturally based Greek life, searching for community on campus, or considering joining SLB, this is a space to learn, listen, and engage. 
 All are welcome.

title: Cru Large Group!
description:
Cru is a caring community passionate about connecting people to Jesus Christ!  Join us in learning together on Who Christ is, building a personal relationship with Him, and joining a community filled with love and open arms!  
 This semester, we will be focusing on the theme  "Identity in Christ."  We pray that our students will learn more about who they are in Christ, and look at some examples of people in the Bible where God revealed their true identity in Christ! 
 " And they devoted themselves to the apostles' teaching and the fellowship, to the breaking of bread and the prayers.   And awe came upon every soul, and many wonders and signs were being done through the apostles.   And all who believed were together and had all things in common.   And they were selling their possessions and belongings and distributing the proceeds to all, as any had need.   And day by day, attending the temple together and breaking bread in their homes, they received their food with glad and generous hearts,  praising God and having favor with all the people. And the Lord added to their number day by day those who were being saved." - Acts 2:42-47

title: Free Swim Lessons *Intermediate*
description:
Take your swimming to the next level! Get introduced to new strokes or work on the ones you already know. These lessons are for swimmers who are a little more comfortable in the water but still want to improve their technique and expand what skills they know.  
   
 Skills Focus: 
 
 Swimming unassisted on your front and back  
 Improve treading (in the deep end) 
 Improving breath control in the water 
 Learn alternate kicks (Dolphin Kick) 
 Introduction to and/or improving your breaststroke 
 Swimming laps 
 Introduction to open and flip turns 
 Introduction to Butterfly 
 
 How do I Sign-up? 
 
 Registration is not required 
 FREE with a valid Montclair State ID 
 
 When and Where? 
 
 Monday: 1-2PM & 5-6PM 
 Student Recreation Center - Pool

title: Free Swim Lessons *Beginner*
description:
Brief Description:  
 Never swam before? No problem! These lessons will give you the important foundational skills in swimming and help you be more comfortable in the pool. Learn how to use proper breath control, float, and swim with a kickboard.  
 Skills Focus: 
 
 Being comfortable in the water 
 Learning how to float on your back and front 
 Learning how to kick off the wall with a kickboard 
 Introducing proper breath control in the water 
 Learning how to tread water 
 Learn alternate kicks (Froggy Kick) 
 Being comfortable in the deep end  
 Introducing arms to your strokes 
 
 How do I Sign-up? 
 
 Registration is not required 
 FREE with a valid Montclair State ID 
 
 When and Where? 
 
 Monday: 1-2PM & 5-6PM

title: Polish Club Smigus Dyngus "Dunk an Exec" Fundraiser
location: Student Center Quad Information Table 1 - Information Table 1
description:
Celebrate Polish tradition with a twist at our Śmigus-Dyngus Fundraiser! Inspired by the Polish holiday where friends splash water on one another to welcome spring and bring good luck, the Polish Club is giving you the chance to dump water on one of our executive board members! 
 For a donation, you’ll get the opportunity to take part in this playful tradition and soak an exec, all for a great cause. Every splash helps us raise money for future cultural events, community service initiatives, and exciting Polish Club programming. 
 Come support, bring your friends, and get ready to make a splash while celebrating Polish culture in the most fun way possible!

title: Polishing and Proofreading Tips for Final Drafts

Learn about our different programs that are as short as one week up to an academic year as well as scholarship information. Whether you want to stay as close to home as Pennsylvania or travel as far away as South Korea, Montclair State University has programs for you.

title: Red Hawk Pantry
description:
Visiting the Pantry: 
 1) Please have your Event Pass, CWID and have a  PantrySoft Registration .  2) Please bring your own shopping bag and ID card! ***Visitors are allowed to come once/week*** 
 Our Mission:   We aim to address food insecurity across Montclair State University. The Red Hawk Pantry is open to all students, staff, faculty, and Alumni at no cost. If you are unable to visit during our regular hours, please reach out via email at redhawkpantry@montclair.edu Stay updated by following us on Instagram: @montclair_redhawkpantry

description: Are you looking to make a difference in the world? Join us for an engaging meeting on how to get involved in local politics. We'll explore current issues, brainstorm creative ways to engage students, and discuss how you can make a real impact on upcoming campaigns and events.

title: Chess Club SB Meeting!
description:
Join us for an evening of strategy, skill, and friendly competition! Whether you’re a seasoned player or just learning the game, all are welcome. Come at your convenience! We’ll have time for casual matches, analysis, and discussion of tactics and openings.  
 Boards and pieces will be provided;  just bring your enthusiasm for the game!

title: 8Th DECA GBM
description:
Join Montclair State University DECA for our weekly General Body Meetings! Each session focuses on professional development, real-world business strategy, and preparation for case competitions. Members will strengthen their skills in marketing, finance, management, entrepreneurship, and public speaking through interactive activities and practical exercises. 
 Whether you’re new to DECA or preparing to compete, our GBMs are designed to help you build confidence, think critically, and develop leadership skills in a collaborative environment. 
 All majors and experience levels are welcome.

All are welcome, whether you have the most craft experience possible or none at all. Come by and enjoy the company of others who share your passions for the fiber arts!

title: BFA Visual Communications Design Graduate Exhibition
description:
The Department of Art & Design invites you to  From the Basement , the BFA Visual Communications Design 2026 Graduate Exhibition. Join us at the Finley Gallery from March 25 to April 7 to experience a dynamic showcase of work by graduating VCDS students, featuring posters, motion graphics, packaging, editorial, and more. With the opening reception on Friday, March 27, 4 to 6pm. 
   
 Venture into the world of graphic design at the VCDS 2026 graduate exhibition,  From the Basement.  This exhibition highlights the work of graduating Visual Communication Design students and the projects they developed throughout their time in the VCDS BFA program. A range of mediums will be featured, including posters, motion graphics, packaging, editorial, and more! Together, these works reflect the unique styles and creative voices of the class. 
   
 Featured Designers: 
 Jalen Aguirre, Ysabella De Leon, Brett Diese, Domenic Falivene, Anna Fronczkowski, Tori Gallagher, Camila Garcia, Robert Gaskill, Britany Giraldo, David Guerra, Audrey Hitchens, Natalia Kurpiewski, Paolo La Rosa, Izzy Latella, Katie Mascuch, Caitlin Mendoza, Evan Okun, Erika Oliva, Julia Oliveri, Christian Pajaro, Nicole Ricci, Christopher Rizzi, Casey Ryu, Tom Sheridan, Michael Shimabukuro, Gabby Trezza, Maral Tutunjian, and Dogan Uludogan 
   
 BFA Visual Communications Design Graduate Exhibition 
 Exhibition dates: March 25–April 7, 2026 
 Opening Reception: Friday, March 27, 4–6pm 
 Finley Gallery, Finley Hall 
   
 Come meet the designers and celebrate their work! Support the next generation of designers as they transition from academia into the professional world.

title: upLIFT
description:
Step into the brand-new PLAE Room and discover upLIFT—a judgement-free program designed to help every student, from complete beginners to seasoned gym-goers, feel confident and capable with free-weight training. Learn proper lifting technique, get personalized form checks, and master the basics of creating your own effective workout programs. Whether you’re looking to meet other students who love to lift, pursue new fitness goals, or simply feel more at home in the weight room, upLIFT offers a supportive community and a dedicated space to grow. The program is structured for steady progression throughout the semester, but you’re welcome to drop in anytime and join at your own pace.

 No registration required 
 FREE for students with a valid Montclair State ID

description:
The  CSAM Science Teaching Program  holds  biweekly meetings  that are open, welcoming, and inclusive! Any student curious about science teaching is invited to join us. Each meeting highlights a theme related to science education, from classroom strategies to career pathways, and offers students opportunities to connect with mentors, explore internships, and gain real-world experience working with teachers and children. Students are encouraged to  bring a guest  who may also be interested in exploring science teaching. 
 These gatherings help us build a strong  community of support , where students encourage one another, share experiences, and access valuable opportunities such as  scholarships, paid internships, and mentoring  from advanced peers and in-service science teachers. Whether you are just beginning to explore the idea of teaching or already on the path toward certification, these meetings are a place to grow, learn, and belong.

title: The DJ Academy: Music Matters 101
description:
DJing is one of the original elements of Hip Hop and is often considered the first. Hip Hop began in the Bronx during the 1970s at community block parties, where DJs used turntables to bring people together through music. Early pioneers like DJ Kool Herc introduced breakbeats by looping the most rhythmic parts of records, creating the foundation for breaking and shaping how dancers and MCs interacted with the music. 
 As Hip Hop evolved, DJing became the musical backbone of the culture. DJs provide the beats for MCing, set the tempo for dancers, and control the energy of Hip Hop spaces by reading the crowd and responding in real time. Through techniques like scratching, cutting, and beat juggling, DJs helped define Hip Hop’s sound while preserving its history and passing down cultural knowledge. 
 This class introduces the basics of DJing and how music shapes the dance experience. DJ Bascis will explain core concepts such as  tempo, rhythm, and song structure , helping students understand how music guides movement and energy on the dance floor. 
 Participants will learn how DJs choose music, create smooth transitions, and adjust the vibe based on the room.  
 Through simple examples and live demonstrations, students will build confidence in listening to music with intention. This can bewhether they are dancing, freestyling, or interested in DJing themselves. 
 This class is designed for  beginners and college students , with no prior DJ or music experience required.

title: NASO General Body Meeting
description: Join us for the Native African Student Organization's General Body Meeting! This is a space where we come together to celebrate and explore the richness of African cultures, while also connecting with other cultures from around the world. At each meeting, we'll engage in lively discussions, learn about Africa’s diverse history, traditions, and current events, and empower each other to understand and embrace our heritage. Whether you're looking to deepen your cultural knowledge, make meaningful connections, or simply have fun with fellow students who share a passion for Africa and global cultures, this is the place to be! Let’s build a supportive community, amplify African voices on campus, and create lasting friendships. All are welcome, come, learn, share, and connect!

title: IDEALS, INTERESTS, AND INTERNATIONAL ENGAGEMENT: THE UNITED STATES IN WORLD AFFAIRS
location: Presentation Hall (College of Communication and Media)
description: America’s role in shaping the global political and economic order has shifted repeatedly over the last 250 years, reflecting the evolution of American interests and longstanding debates regarding the role of the United States in world affairs. Drawing on foundational principles, pivotal historical moments, and contemporary challenges, this lecture explores how American power, values, and institutions have influenced international norms, security, and governance and also how international dynamics have shaped American democracy. The lecture considers tensions between ideals and practices – democracy, markets, sovereignty, nation-building, imperialism, isolationism, intervention – in U.S. engagement abroad. Participants will be invited to reflect on the ways in which American leadership on the global stage has shaped our nation and what that implies in the current era.

title: Free Swim Lessons *Beginner*
description:
Never swam before? No problem! These lessons will give you the important foundational skills in swimming and help you be more comfortable in the pool. Learn how to use proper breath control, float, and swim with a kickboard.  
 Skills Focus: 
 
 Being comfortable in the water 
 Learning how to float on your back and front 
 Learning how to kick off the wall with a kickboard 
 Introducing proper breath control in the water 
 Learning how to tread water 
 Learn alternate kicks (Froggy Kick) 
 Being comfortable in the deep end  
 Introducing arms to your strokes 
 
 How do I Sign-up? 
 
 Registration is not required 
 FREE with a valid Montclair State ID 
 
 When and Where? 
 
 Thursday: 4-5PM 
 Student Recreation Center - Pool

title: Free Swim Lessons *Intermediate*
description:
Take your swimming to the next level! Get introduced to new strokes or work on the ones you already know. These lessons are for swimmers who are a little more comfortable in the water but still want to improve their technique and expand what skills they know.  
   
 Skills Focus: 
 
 Swimming unassisted on your front and back  
 Improve treading (in the deep end) 
 Improving breath control in the water 
 Learn alternate kicks (Dolphin Kick) 
 Introduction to and/or improving your breaststroke 
 Swimming laps 
 Introduction to open and flip turns 
 Introduction to Butterfly 
 
 How do I Sign-up? 
 
 Registration is not required 
 FREE with a valid Montclair State ID 
 
 When and Where? 
 
 Thursday: 4-5PM 
 Student Recreation Center - Pool

title: Understanding Rights and Responsibilities— Immigration Policy
description:
As immigration policies develop, it is best to stay educated. Know your rights and responsibilities! 
 Join the New Jersey Consortium for Immigrant Children for a webinar on key legal and policy issues impacting the Montclair community. Information will be provided on immigration statuses, policy updates, legal protections, judicial vs. administrative orders, and understanding your rights if encountering law enforcement. Sessions are open for students, family, faculty, and staff in both English and Spanish.  
 The New Jersey Consortium for Immigrant Children (NJCIC) is a state-wide legal and policy advocacy and education organization specializing in youth immigration. They provide objective legal resources, aid, and advice on immigration rights. 
 Student & Family English Session: March 25, 2:30-3:45:  https://us06web.zoom.us/webinar/register/WN_FxYlh_kDRkCYCoOhfmEKyA#/registration 
 Student & Family Spanish Session: April 9, 5:30-6:45:  https://us06web.zoom.us/j/82724283081?pwd=KzAjQw24vxqmzbURBXGmbBLV3V60m3.1

title: Cru Large Group!
description:
Cru is a caring community passionate about connecting people to Jesus Christ!  Join us in learning together on Who Christ is, building a personal relationship with Him, and joining a community filled with love and open arms!  
 This semester, we will be focusing on the theme  "Identity in Christ."  We pray that our students will learn more about who they are in Christ, and look at some examples of people in the Bible where God revealed their true identity in Christ! 
 " And they devoted themselves to the apostles' teaching and the fellowship, to the breaking of bread and the prayers.   And awe came upon every soul, and many wonders and signs were being done through the apostles.   And all who believed were together and had all things in common.   And they were selling their possessions and belongings and distributing the proceeds to all, as any had need.   And day by day, attending the temple together and breaking bread in their homes, they received their food with glad and generous hearts,  praising God and having favor with all the people. And the Lord added to their number day by day those who were being saved." - Acts 2:42-47

location: University Hall 1030 - Charlotte Roome Shotmeyer Lecture Hall
description: A New Member Education (NME) meeting is a structured gathering designed to help new members learn about Tri Sigma’s history, values, traditions, and expectations. During these meetings, new members build connections with one another, develop leadership and personal growth skills, and gain a deeper understanding of what it means to be a lifelong member of the organization. title: CALI SCHOOL OF MUSIC | Viola Masterclass with Melissa Reardon
description:
Viola Masterclass with Melissa Reardon 
 Free and Open to the Public 
 Grammy-nominated violist  Melissa Reardon   of the  Borromeo String Quartet  will lead a masterclass in viola performance and chamber music collaboration, and expressive string  interpretation. 
 Bio 
 Grammy-nominated violist  Melissa Reardon  is an internationally renowned performer whose solo and chamber playing spans all musical genres. Melissa is the Artistic Director of the  Portland Chamber Music Festival  in Portland, ME, violist of the  Borromeo String Quartet , Artist-in-Residence at  Bard College and Conservatory,  and a founding member and Executive Director of the  East Coast Chamber Orchestra (ECCO).

title: Summer Lifeguard Hiring
description:
Campus Recreation is hiring for the summer! 
 We want qualified staff to work during the summer at our 3 facilities on campus.  
 Not certified? We can train you!  
 If you work full time, we provide housing for free at the Village apartments! 
 Apply today 
 Applications due by  4/10! 
 All employees must be current students, registered for Fall 2026. Incoming students can be hired starting July 1st. We offer part-time and full-time hours at both indoor and outdoor facilities. We will work around class schedules, but all staff must be able to work a minimum of 14 hours (part-time), and 32 hours (full-time). Full time staff get free housing at the Village. 
 Currently certified lifeguards - we accept all major certifications, and can recertify you for free.  
 Non-certified students - Students that are not currently certified can take a pretest for free, before you register for a lifeguard training course. If you pass the pretest, and can work a minimum of 14 hours per week for the summer (average), than we will certify you for free. If you can work full-time, we may also be able to offer free housing for the summer (space is limited, so this option is first-come, first-serve). 
 Swim tests will take place between March 23rd and April 10th, 2026.

title: Tri Sigma Initiation
description: Tri Sigma initiation is a meaningful ceremony that marks the transition from new member to fully initiated sister within the sorority. It is a formal and reflective experience centered on the organization’s values of wisdom, power, faith, hope, and love. During initiation, members learn more deeply about Tri Sigma’s traditions, history, and lifelong commitment to sisterhood, service, and personal growth. The ceremony symbolizes belonging, responsibility, and the shared bond that connects all Tri Sigma members across chapters and generations.

title: Bearfort Mountain (Advanced)
description:
Join Outdoor Adventure for one of the most strenuous but spectacular hikes of the New Jersey and New York area. As we hike the Bearfort Mountain ridge past the Greenwood Lake, rhododendron tunnel, surprise lake, and west pond. Sure to be a breathtaking view the whole way up to the summit of the mountain.  
   
 How do I sign-up? 
 
 Register in the Student Recreation Center main office, on Fusion, or over the phone (973-655-3340). 
 Cost for Students  $10; Faculty/Staff $20 
 
   
 Where and when? 
 
 Saturday, April 11, 2026 
 10:00am –5:00pm 
 Begins at Student Recreation Center 
 
   
 What to bring:  Water, lunch, weather appropriate clothing, boots ( sneakers not acceptable ), lunch, snacks, and sunscreen. 
   
 Potential Hazards:  Unpredictable weather changes, sunburn/heat exhaustion, dehydration, allergic reactions/poisonous plants, insect bites and injury by running, including sprains, lacerations, abrasions and fractures.

title: Spring Retreat
description:
Join Campus Activities for our Spring Student Retreat  a chance to step away from campus, connect with other students, and enjoy a day filled with activities, good food, and a refreshing change of scenery. This trip is designed to help you relax, recharge, and make new memories while participating in group experiences and social opportunities throughout the day. 
 The trip is open to current Bloomfield/Montclair State students only. Spots are limited and available on a first-come, first-served basis. 
 A  $5 (cash only) reservation fee  is required to secure your spot at the time of sign-up. 
 Transportation will be provided, and the bus will depart from and return to the Bloomfield Campus. All meals are included, and some snacks will be provided. Students are encouraged to wear comfortable clothing and shoes suitable for a full day of activities. 
 Additional trip details, including itinerary information and preparation instructions, will be shared with students once their registration and reservation are confirmed. 
   
 To sign up you must go to the Bloomfield Campus - Campus Activities located in Talbott Hall

title: Croton Aqueduct Bike Trip
description:
Get ready for a scenic spin through history as we ride the legendary Old Croton Aqueduct! This unforgettable bike trip takes you along one of New York’s most beautiful and peaceful trails, where tree-lined paths, and charming river views, create the perfect backdrop for a day outdoors. As we cruise along the trail you’ll enjoy plenty of photo-worthy stops along the way. Expect a relaxed pace with time to explore, snack, and soak in the scenery. Whether you're in it for the fresh air, the history, or just a joyful ride with great company, this Croton Aqueduct adventure is a perfect blend of nature, culture, and fun on two wheels!  
 How do I sign-up? 
 
 Register online through  Fusion , in the Student Recreation Center's main office, or over the phone (973-655-3340). 
 Cost for Students - $10 | Faculty/Staff - $20 
 
 When and Where? 
 
 Sunday, April 12, 2026 
 10:00 am - 5:00 pm 
 Croton Aqueduct, NJ - Begins and ends at Student Recreation Center 
 
 What to bring: 
 
 Money for spending, lunch, water, comfortable shoes, sunscreen. 
 
 Potential Hazards:  Hazardous weather, poisonous plants/allergies, insect bites, unpredictable weather changes, sunburn/heat exhaustion, dehydration, and injuries, including sprains/strains, abrasions, lacerations and fractures.

title: CALI SCHOOL OF MUSIC: Faculty Recital - David Witten, pianist - “From Sea to Shining Sea”
description: “From Sea to Shining Sea” Music to celebrate America's 250th!   This concert will feature two composers who were personal friends with GEORGE WASHINGTON.   David Witten,  pianist with Grace Renée Pfleger,  mezzo-soprano   Music of  Alexander Reinagle, Mario Castelnuovo-Tedesco, Francis Hopkinson, Judith Lang Zaimont, Charles Ives , and  Louis Moreau Gottschalk     Admission is free No reservations required

title: Community and Social Services Career Expo
organization: College of Humanities and Social Sciences (CHSS)
description:
Community and Social Services Career Expo Date:  April 13, 2026 Time:  3:30pm-6:30pm Location:  University Hall Conference Center (7th Floor) 
 Registration is available on Handshake:  https://app.joinhandshake.com/career_fairs/37dfb726-9582-4e23-9c82-6561adbc3e11/student_preview 
   
 Description: 
 Calling all community and service-minded students exploring clinical and non-clinical career options! 
   
 Are you looking for a job or internship? Are you curious about the career paths you can pursue with your major or graduate program? If so, the Community and Social Services Career Expo is an amazing opportunity to meet employers that align with your professional interests. Whether you are a freshman or a graduate student, this event is designed to help you explore career possibilities, gain networking experience by talking with employers, and make connections that may eventually lead to a job or internship. There will be fun mixed in with the opportunity to win raffle prizes!  
   
 More Information: - Mark your calendar! Monday, April 13, 3:30pm - 6:30pm, University Hall Conference Center (7th Floor) - Visit with non-profit, government, and corporate employers that have a service-oriented mission - Discover opportunities with campus organizations and departments, including international experiences that incorporate social impact - Pre-registration is strongly encouraged -  Register on Handshake! - Event details and employer list can be found on  Handshake .  - Dress neatly and bring resumes! Check out  Rocky's Closet  for free professional attire.  
   
 This event is ideal for graduate and undergraduate students seeking jobs and internships in: Counseling, Child Advocacy, Justice Studies, Social Work, Political Science, Psychology and so much more! 
   
 Co-sponsored by:   College of Humanities and Social Sciences, College for Community Health, Office of Community Engagement and Partnerships, Office of Experiential Education and Career Connections, Office of Government Relations, Office of International Academic Initiatives 
   
 Questions? Contact Jennifer Dudeck-Lenis at  dudecklenisj@montclair.edu.

title: upLIFT
description:
Step into the brand-new PLAE Room and discover upLIFT—a judgement-free program designed to help every student, from complete beginners to seasoned gym-goers, feel confident and capable with free-weight training. Learn proper lifting technique, get personalized form checks, and master the basics of creating your own effective workout programs. Whether you’re looking to meet other students who love to lift, pursue new fitness goals, or simply feel more at home in the weight room, upLIFT offers a supportive community and a dedicated space to grow. The program is structured for steady progression throughout the semester, but you’re welcome to drop in anytime and join at your own pace.

 No registration required
